schools.summary: Paul, having exalted love in ch. 13, bids them 'follow after' (pursue earnestly) charity, making it the first object and atmosphere of all gift-use; they may desire spiritual gifts (charismata), but especially prophesying — the gift of edifying inspired speech in the known tongue, superior to tongues for the church's good (Barnes, Robertson).

Chrysostom (with Henry): love is to be pursued above all, and among gifts prophecy preferred; the chapter's whole aim is to subordinate the showy gift of tongues to the edifying gift of prophecy, all governed by love.

themes: Follow after charity, and desire spiritual gifts
but rather that ye may prophesy

schools.summary: 'Tongue' denotes the power of speaking a foreign language (as at Pentecost, Acts 2), 'unknown' supplied because unknown to the hearers; since no one present understands, the speaker addresses God (who understands all) rather than men, who derive no instruction; the gift exercised without interpretation profits the assembly nothing (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): 'in the spirit he speaketh mysteries' — by the Spirit (or in his own spirit) he utters truths hidden from the hearers because unintelligible to them; the gift, though real, is unfruitful without interpretation.

themes: he that speaketh in an unknown tongue speaketh not unto men, but unto God
no man understandeth him; howbeit in the spirit he speaketh mysteries
the tongue not understood by men

schools.summary: By contrast with tongues, the prophet (one who speaks intelligibly under inspiration) 'speaketh unto men' to their profit, in three ways: edification (building up in faith), exhortation (stirring to duty), and comfort (consolation in trial) — the very things the assembly needs and that unintelligible tongues cannot give (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: because prophecy is understood, it actually builds, rouses, and consoles the church — proving it the greater gift for public worship.

themes: he that prophesieth speaketh unto men to edification, and exhortation, and comfort

schools.summary: The decisive comparison: the tongue-speaker 'edifieth himself' only — he may be inwardly moved and benefited, but the assembly gains nothing; the prophet 'edifieth the church' — the whole body is built up; in public worship the aim is the edification of all, which tongues alone do not serve (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): the worth of a public gift is measured by its usefulness to the church; prophecy, edifying all, is therefore preferable to tongues, which edify only the speaker.

themes: He that speaketh in an unknown tongue edifieth himself
he that prophesieth edifieth the church
prophecy edifies the church, tongues only oneself
the measure of a public gift

schools.summary: Paul does not despise tongues — he would be glad if all had the gift — but prefers that they prophesy, the more useful gift; the prophet is 'greater' (more useful in the church) than the tongue-speaker, unless the latter also interprets, so that the church may understand and be edified; interpretation puts tongues on a level with prophecy for usefulness (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): edification ('that the church may receive edifying') is the measure throughout; whatever builds up the church is to be preferred.

themes: I would that ye all spake with tongues, but rather that ye prophesied
greater is he that prophesieth than he that speaketh with tongues, except he interpret

schools.summary: Paul applies the principle to himself: if even he came to them speaking only in tongues, he would profit them nothing, unless he also spoke intelligibly — by revelation (a truth disclosed), knowledge (understanding imparted), prophesying (inspired edifying speech), or doctrine (teaching), all conveyed so as to be understood (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin: the gift is worthless to hearers unless it conveys understanding; even an apostle's tongues, uninterpreted, would not edify the church.

themes: if I come unto you speaking with tongues, what shall I profit you
except I shall speak... by revelation, or by knowledge, or by prophesying, or by doctrine

schools.summary: Paul argues from lifeless instruments (the pipe/flute and harp): even they must give 'a distinction in the sounds' — distinct, recognizable notes — or no one can tell what tune is played; if even instruments must produce intelligible, distinguishable sounds to convey a tune, how much more must human speech be intelligible to convey meaning (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: confused, indistinct sounds (whether of instruments or of tongues) convey nothing; clarity is essential to communication.

themes: things without life giving sound, whether pipe or harp
except they give a distinction in the sounds, how shall it be known what is piped or harped

schools.summary: A second musical illustration: the war-trumpet, whose distinct calls signal the army; if it gives 'an uncertain sound' (indistinct, ambiguous), no soldier can know whether to prepare for battle, advance, or retreat — an unclear signal producing confusion and unreadiness, clarity being essential where action depends on the message (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: as an indistinct trumpet fails its purpose, so unintelligible tongues fail to rouse or instruct the church; the message must be clear to have effect.

themes: if the trumpet give an uncertain sound, who shall prepare himself to the battle
an uncertain sound fails its purpose

schools.summary: Paul applies the musical illustrations: 'so likewise ye' — unless they utter 'words easy to be understood' (intelligible, plain), no one will know what is spoken, and they speak in vain; 'into the air' is a vivid image of unintelligible speech wasted, reaching no understanding, accomplishing nothing (Barnes, Robertson).
Wesley: the whole point of speech is to convey meaning; tongues without interpretation, however impressive, are empty sound spoken into the air.

themes: except ye utter by the tongue words easy to be understood, how shall it be known what is spoken
ye shall speak into the air
intelligible words or speaking into the air

schools.summary: Paul observes that there are many kinds of 'voices' (languages, tongues) in the world, and none is 'without signification' — each language conveys real meaning to those who know it; since every language is meaningful to its own speakers, a language is useful only where it is understood, meaning depending on the hearer's comprehension (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: languages are not empty sounds but vehicles of meaning — yet meaning unshared is useless; the gift of tongues, to profit, requires that the meaning be conveyed.

themes: so many kinds of voices in the world, and none of them is without signification

schools.summary: Paul draws the inference: if he does not know the meaning of a language ('the voice'), then he and the speaker are mutual 'barbarians' — the Greek term for a foreigner whose speech is unintelligible; without shared understanding, speaker and hearer are as strangers to one another, communication failing entirely (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: so it is in the church when tongues are used without interpretation — speaker and hearers are mutually estranged, the assembly profiting nothing.

themes: if I know not the meaning of the voice, I shall be unto him that speaketh a barbarian

schools.summary: Paul turns their very zeal to good account: 'forasmuch as ye are zealous' (eager, ambitious) for spiritual gifts, let them direct that zeal rightly — to 'excel to the edifying of the church,' to abound in gifts useful for building up the body; edification of the church being the measure by which gifts are to be valued and sought (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul does not quench their zeal but channels it — seek to abound, but in the gifts that build up the church, not in the showy gift of tongues.

themes: forasmuch as ye are zealous of spiritual gifts, seek that ye may excel to the edifying of the church
seek gifts that edify the church
edification the goal of holy ambition

schools.summary: The practical instruction: one who has the gift of tongues should 'pray that he may interpret' — seek (by prayer) the further gift of interpretation, so that what he speaks may be made intelligible and edify the church; tongues become useful when interpreted (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the gift of tongues, to be of any public use, must be joined to interpretation; hence the speaker is to seek that gift, that his tongue may serve the church.

themes: let him that speaketh in an unknown tongue pray that he may interpret

schools.summary: If one prays in a tongue, 'my spirit prayeth' — his own inner being (or the Spirit's gift in him) is engaged and devout, but only privately; while the 'understanding' (nous, the rational mind) is 'unfruitful,' bearing no fruit for others, so the prayer profits neither the assembly nor fully himself (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): true worship engages both the affections (spirit) and the understanding; prayer in a tongue, lacking the latter's fruit, is defective for public use.

themes: if I pray in an unknown tongue, my spirit prayeth, but my understanding is unfruitful

schools.summary: Paul resolves the matter: he will pray 'with the spirit' (with devout affection, or the Spirit's gift) and 'with the understanding also' (intelligibly, so the mind is engaged and others edified), both together; the same applying to singing — praise too engaging both the heart's devotion and the intelligent understanding, in a known tongue (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Henry): acceptable public worship — whether prayer or praise — requires the union of fervent spirit and enlightened understanding; neither devotion without intelligence nor intelligence without devotion suffices.

themes: I will pray with the spirit, and I will pray with the understanding also
I will sing with the spirit, and with the understanding also

schools.summary: If one gives thanks only 'with the spirit' (in a tongue, without the understanding), the worship is not shared; 'he that occupieth the room (place) of the unlearned' (the ordinary worshipper) cannot say the 'Amen' — the assenting response by which the congregation makes the prayer its own — because he does not understand what is said (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): public prayer is to be intelligible so that the people may join in it and assent with the 'Amen'; unintelligible prayer excludes the congregation from worship.

themes: how shall he that occupieth the room of the unlearned say Amen at thy giving of thanks
seeing he understandeth not what thou sayest
the unlearned unable to say Amen

schools.summary: Paul concedes the tongue-speaker 'givest thanks well' — his thanksgiving may be genuine, devout, and good in itself — 'but the other is not edified,' the hearer gains nothing; however excellent the prayer in itself, if it is unintelligible the other worshipper is not built up, and the public good is not served, for in public worship edification of the hearer is the test (Barnes, Robertson).

themes: thou verily givest thanks well, but the other is not edified
good thanksgiving failing to edify others

schools.summary: Paul forestalls the charge that he disparages tongues from lacking the gift: on the contrary, he thanks God that he himself speaks with tongues more than all of them — he possesses the gift abundantly; this gives weight to his counsel, as one not envious or deficient but richly endowed with the very gift he subordinates to prophecy (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul does not condemn tongues but their misuse; his own large possession of the gift proves he values it rightly, while still preferring edification.

themes: I thank my God, I speak with tongues more than ye all
not disparaging the gift but its misuse

schools.summary: Paul's striking comparison: in the assembly he would rather speak five intelligible words (that teach others) than ten thousand in an unknown tongue — the proportion (five to ten thousand) dramatizing the worthlessness of unintelligible speech for public use; the intelligible words teach and edify the hearers, the value of public speech lying in its usefulness to all (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Wesley): in the church, the briefest intelligible instruction infinitely outweighs the most copious unintelligible utterance; edification, not display, is the measure.

themes: I had rather speak five words with my understanding... than ten thousand words in an unknown tongue
that by my voice I might teach others also
a few intelligible words over many in a tongue

schools.summary: Paul gently rebukes their childishness: in prizing the showy gift of tongues over the useful gift of prophecy, they were acting like children, drawn to the impressive rather than the profitable; the wordplay urging them to be like children in 'malice' (evil, vice) — innocent, free from guile — but 'men' in 'understanding' (judgment, sense), mature and discerning (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Chrysostom): childlike innocence in regard to evil is good, but childish judgment in spiritual matters is to be put away; maturity of understanding would correct their misplaced zeal for tongues.

themes: in malice be ye children, but in understanding be men
children in malice, men in understanding

schools.summary: Paul quotes Isaiah 28:11-12 ('the law' used broadly for the Old Testament): God would speak to his rebellious people 'with men of other tongues' — through foreign-speaking conquerors (the Assyrians), a sign of judgment; yet even this strange speech did not bring the people to obedience ('they will not hear'), the tongues serving as a token of judgment on unbelief, not a means of convincing the disobedient (Barnes, Robertson).

Wesley: the original tongues were a sign to a rebellious people who still would not hear; this prepares for Paul's point (v.22) that tongues are a sign to unbelievers, not the edifying gift for the church.

themes: With men of other tongues... will I speak unto this people; and yet... will they not hear me
tongues a sign that did not convert the rebellious

schools.summary: Tongues are a miraculous sign meant to convince unbelievers of the truth of the gospel — an evidence (which God alone could give) that the religion is from God, designed to aid in its propagation (as at Pentecost); prophecy is chiefly for believers, to edify the church, not so striking a proof to gainsayers but better adapted to nurture the faithful (Barnes, Clarke, Wesley).

Calvin (with Gill): drawing on v.21's Isaiah quote, tongues are a sign of judgment upon unbelievers — a punishment by which God inflicts vengeance on the rebellious, not properly a blessing bestowed on believers; Calvin allows the alternative that, as a miracle, they served to prepare those at a distance for obedience, but the Corinthians prized them out of place, neglecting prophecy.

debates.question: In what sense are tongues 'a sign to them that believe not'?

sides.position: A miraculous sign to convince unbelievers that the gospel is from God
A sign of judgment/punishment upon the rebellious unbelieving (from the Isaiah quote, v.21)

themes: tongues are for a sign, not to them that believe, but to them that believe not

schools.summary: Paul pictures the consequence of their disorder: if the whole assembly all spoke in tongues at once, and an 'unlearned' person (a plain inquirer) or 'unbeliever' came in, he would find only unintelligible babble and conclude they were 'mad' (insane, raving) — far from convincing the outsider, such confusion would repel him, the gift abused bringing the gospel into contempt (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the very gift meant (in some sense) for outsiders, when used indiscriminately, drives them away; disorder discredits the worship.

themes: if... all speak with tongues, and there come in those that are unlearned, or unbelievers, will they not say that ye are mad
indiscriminate tongues making the church seem mad

schools.summary: The opposite effect of prophecy: if all (in turn) prophesy intelligibly, and an unbeliever or inquirer comes in, 'he is convinced of all' — convicted, his sin and need brought home by the plain, searching truth he hears; 'judged of all' meaning examined, searched, brought under scrutiny, the intelligible word probing his conscience and exposing his true state (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin: intelligible prophecy reaches the heart, convicting and humbling the hearer — the very effect that unintelligible tongues cannot produce; this proves prophecy the more useful even toward outsiders.

themes: if all prophesy, and there come in one that believeth not... he is convinced of all, he is judged of all
the searching power of intelligible truth

schools.summary: The result of prophecy's searching power: the hidden things of the hearer's heart are laid bare — he feels his secret sins and thoughts exposed by the truth, as though the speaker knew him; convicted and humbled, he falls down in worship, acknowledging God's presence and power, and confesses that God is truly among them — the very conversion the gift of tongues could not produce (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the convinced unbeliever testifies that God is truly present among them — the intelligible word having revealed the divine presence in the assembly.

themes: the secrets of his heart made manifest
falling down on his face he will worship God, and report that God is in you of a truth

schools.summary: Paul describes the actual confusion: each member, eager to display his gift, came with something to contribute — a psalm, a teaching, a tongue, a revelation, an interpretation — all pressing forward at once, in disorder; and lays down the governing rule, 'let all things be done unto edifying,' every exercise of gift aiming at building up the church, not at self-display (Barnes, Robertson, Henry).
Calvin: edification is the test and the regulator of all that is done in worship; whatever does not build up is out of place.

themes: every one of you hath a psalm... hath an interpretation
Let all things be done unto edifying

schools.summary: Paul's specific rules for tongues in the assembly: only two, or at most three, are to speak in a tongue at any meeting, and 'by course' (in turn, one after another) — not all at once, not endlessly; and one is to interpret what is spoken, so that the tongue, otherwise useless, may edify the church (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: these limits curb the disorder and ensure that even tongues, when used, serve edification through interpretation; the gift is regulated, not forbidden.

themes: let it be by two, or at the most by three, and that by course

schools.summary: The firm direction: if no interpreter is present, the tongue-speaker is to 'keep silence in the church' — not to speak in a tongue at all, since it would not edify; the gift may still be used privately, 'to himself, and to God,' in inward or solitary devotion, but the public assembly is not the place for it without interpretation (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: the public exercise of any gift is bound by the rule of edification; what cannot edify the church is to be kept private.

themes: if there be no interpreter, let him keep silence in the church
let him speak to himself, and to God

schools.summary: The parallel rule for prophecy: let the prophets (those with the gift) speak 'two or three' at a meeting — an orderly number, not all at once — and 'let the other judge' (let the rest weigh, discern, evaluate what is spoken), testing the prophecies whether they are of God and true; the gift is not exempt from examination (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Cyril): prophecy is to be subject to discernment, that error or pretended inspiration may be detected; the church is to weigh what is taught by the rule of Scripture.

themes: Let the prophets speak two or three, and let the other judge

schools.summary: The orderly direction: if, while one is speaking, something is revealed to another who is seated, the one speaking ('the first') is to 'hold his peace' (stop, give way) — that the new revelation may be heard, one at a time; the prophets are to defer to one another, the gift exercised in turn with consideration (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: even inspired speech is to be governed by decorum and mutual yielding; the Spirit's gifts do not justify confusion.

themes: If any thing be revealed to another that sitteth by, let the first hold his peace
a new revelation yields the floor

schools.summary: The reason for the rule of yielding: all who have the gift may prophesy, but 'one by one' (in turn, successively) — so that the orderly exercise allows all the hearers to 'learn' (be instructed) and 'be comforted' (consoled, encouraged); the orderly successive prophesying serving the whole assembly's instruction and consolation (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: order is not the enemy but the servant of edification; by speaking in turn, the gifts profit all, where confusion would profit none.

themes: ye may all prophesy one by one, that all may learn, and all may be comforted

schools.summary: The ground of the rule of order: 'the spirits of the prophets are subject to the prophets' — the prophetic impulse (or the gift) is under the control of the prophet himself; he is not seized by an irresistible frenzy but can govern his exercise, speaking or keeping silence as order requires, and so is responsible to observe order, unable to excuse confusion by pleading uncontrollable inspiration (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: unlike the pagan oracles, who raved beyond self-control, the true gifts of the Spirit leave the prophet master of himself, able to submit to decorum.

themes: the spirits of the prophets are subject to the prophets
the gift under the prophet's control

schools.summary: The great ground of order: God is 'not the author of confusion' (disorder, tumult, akatastasia) — the disorderly worship of the Corinthians could not be from him, for his nature and his Spirit produce order, not chaos; rather he is the author 'of peace,' of harmony, concord, and calm order, so that worship reflecting his character will be peaceable and orderly (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: 'as in all churches of the saints' — the practice of all the churches confirms it; orderly, peaceable worship is the universal rule, and the Corinthians' confusion an aberration.

themes: God is not the author of confusion, but of peace
as in all churches of the saints

schools.summary: Paul forbids women to 'speak' in the public assembly — to teach, lead, or address the congregation authoritatively; the silence enjoined concerns public, authoritative speaking, grounded in the order of subjection ('as saith the law,' Genesis 3:16). Reconciled with 11:5 (women praying or prophesying) by holding the prohibition is of authoritative public teaching and disorderly speaking, or that 11:5 reproved only the manner (Barnes, Robertson, Gill).

Chrysostom: Paul represses the women's unseasonable boldness with much authority, citing the law ('thy desire shall be to thy husband, and he shall rule over thee,' Genesis 3:16); not merely silence but subjection — what they wish to learn, let them ask at home; if even the gifted must speak in turn, much more the women who prate idly.

Henry: Paul enjoins silence so far that women must not even ask questions in the church but ask their husbands at home (cf. 1 Timothy 2:11-12); the intimation of 11:5 that women sometimes prayed or prophesied he does not there simply condemn but the manner (uncovered), while here he seems to forbid all public performances of theirs in the assembly.

debates.question: Is the silence enjoined on women absolute, or limited to authoritative teaching / disorderly speaking (consistent with 11:5)?

sides.position: A real prohibition of women teaching or speaking authoritatively (and even asking questions) in the assembly
Limited to authoritative public teaching or disorderly speaking, not contradicting the veiled praying/prophesying of 11:5

themes: Let your women keep silence in the churches
they are commanded to be under obedience, as also saith the law

schools.summary: Paul provides for the women's instruction: if they wish to learn or have questions, they are to ask their own husbands at home — not to interrupt or speak in the public assembly; the ground being propriety, it being 'a shame' (unbecoming, disgraceful) in that setting for women to speak (address, hold forth) in the public assembly, contrary to the modesty and order befitting them (Barnes, Robertson, Gill).

Chrysostom (with Calvin): the rule preserves decorum and the order of subjection; the women's instruction is provided for, but in a way consistent with their place — what they would learn, let them ask at home.

themes: if they will learn any thing, let them ask their husbands at home
it is a shame for women to speak in the church
the unseemliness of women speaking publicly

schools.summary: Paul rebukes their independent spirit: did the word of God originate 'out from you' (from Corinth)? or did it come 'unto you only' (to Corinth alone)? — exposing the arrogance of their thinking themselves free to set their own customs against the practice of all the churches; the questions humble their pride, since they were neither the source nor the sole recipients of the gospel (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): the Corinthians' self-willed innovations (in tongues, in the women's speaking) are checked by reminding them they are but one church among many, bound by the common order.

themes: came the word of God out from you? or came it unto you only

schools.summary: Paul addresses those who claimed to be prophets or spiritually gifted — the very ones likely to resist his regulations — and lays down the decisive test: the truly spiritual will recognize that Paul's directions are not his own opinions but 'the commandments of the Lord,' divinely authoritative; submission to apostolic teaching is the mark of genuine spirituality (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul asserts the inspired, binding character of his instructions; to reject them is to prove oneself not truly spiritual, whatever one's claims.

themes: If any man think himself to be a prophet, or spiritual
let him acknowledge that the things that I write unto you are the commandments of the Lord

schools.summary: Paul's solemn close to the appeal: if anyone is 'ignorant' (refuses to recognize the authority of his command, persists in not knowing), then 'let him be ignorant' — Paul leaves him to his chosen ignorance, refusing to argue further; not indifference but a grave warning, the one who wilfully rejects apostolic authority left to his own obstinacy (some texts read 'he is not recognized' by God, Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: where a man persists in refusing plain, divinely authorized instruction, further reasoning is useless; he is abandoned to his self-chosen blindness.

themes: if any man be ignorant, let him be ignorant
the warning to the wilfully ignorant
the obstinate left to his ignorance

schools.summary: Paul's summary conclusion: 'covet (earnestly desire) to prophesy' — pursue the more useful gift — yet 'forbid not to speak with tongues' — do not prohibit the genuine gift of tongues, rightly used; the whole chapter has regulated, not banned, tongues, prophecy being the greater gift but tongues not to be despised where exercised in order and with interpretation (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin: Paul neither exalts tongues unduly (as the Corinthians did) nor suppresses them entirely; he gives each gift its proper place under the rule of edification.

themes: covet to prophesy, and forbid not to speak with tongues

schools.summary: Paul closes the whole discussion of worship with a comprehensive rule: 'let all things be done decently (becomingly, with seemliness) and in order (according to a fitting arrangement, without confusion)' — the governing standard for all that is done in the assembly; the two marks being propriety (becoming God's worship) and regularity (each thing in its place), the opposite of the Corinthian confusion (Barnes, Robertson).

Calvin (with Wesley): this principle governs not only the matters Paul has addressed but the whole conduct of public worship in every age; decency and order are abiding requirements of Christian assembly.

themes: Let all things be done decently and in order
the great rule of decency and order
the abiding standard for the assembly
